title: How to Launch a Self-Learning Academy
excerpt: A practical checklist for packaging recorded lessons, resources, certificates, and checkout into a clean academy launch.
content:
A self-learning academy works best when every course has a clear promise, structured chapters, downloadable support material, and a certificate path learners can understand.

Start with three to five flagship courses, keep the navigation simple, and make the student dashboard the center of the learning experience.

title: What Makes a Course Page Convert
excerpt: Use outcomes, curriculum, certificate details, pricing, and FAQs to help learners decide with confidence.
content:
Good course pages answer the learner's biggest questions quickly: what they will learn, how long it takes, what is included, and what proof they receive after completion.

Keep the curriculum visible, show a useful thumbnail, and place pricing and discounts where buyers naturally expect them.
imageAlt: Learner reviewing course details on laptop

title: Simple Certificate Rules for Starter Academies
excerpt: Keep certification easy to understand by connecting it directly to lesson completion and progress.
content:
Starter academies do not need complex eligibility logic. A clean certificate flow can unlock after required lessons are completed, with a template that carries learner name, course name, and completion date.

This keeps the promise clear for students and reduces support questions for admins.

title: Choosing Demo Courses for a Marketplace Preview
excerpt: Use a varied course catalog so buyers can imagine the software in different education niches.
content:
A good marketplace demo should include technical, creative, communication, wellness, and business courses. The variety shows how thumbnails, categories, tags, pricing, and certificates work together.

Each demo course should include chapters and sample lessons so the admin and student screens feel complete.
imageAlt: Course catalog cards in a grid

title: Coupons That Help Without Confusing Buyers
excerpt: Use clear welcome discounts, course-specific offers, and limited campaigns to keep checkout simple.
content:
Coupons should be easy for learners to understand. A welcome discount, a featured course offer, and a seasonal campaign are enough for most starter academies.

Admins should be able to see the coupon rules without needing a complicated promotion engine.
imageAlt: Checkout promotion and course purchase screen

title: Designing a Student Dashboard That Feels Useful
excerpt: My courses, progress, certificates, and profile settings are the core pieces learners expect.
content:
The dashboard should help students resume learning quickly. Put enrolled courses, completion status, certificates, and profile controls where learners can find them without friction.

For a starter kit, clarity matters more than adding every possible learning feature.

title: Keeping Admin Settings Simple
excerpt: Starter products feel better when branding, SMTP, storage, payments, and CMS controls are focused.
content:
Admin settings should cover the essentials: academy identity, logos, contact information, SMTP, storage, payment keys, and basic page content.

Removing advanced enterprise-only toggles keeps setup faster and makes the product easier to sell.
imageAlt: Admin settings and brand setup screen

title: A Clean Installer Builds Trust
excerpt: License activation, database setup, academy details, admin creation, SMTP, storage, and demo import should feel guided.
content:
The installer is the first real product experience for a buyer. It should validate the license, connect the database, collect academy details, create the admin account, configure essentials, and optionally import demo data.

A guided setup reduces support load and helps buyers reach a working academy faster.
